Dear, the leaves are like this; what's wrong?

This tomato branch tip is showing a mixed problem of bacterial disease (such as bacterial pith necrosis) + physiological nutrient deficiency, the core cause is high humidity and stuffiness + nutrient imbalance.
Is there a way to save it?

Core symptoms correspondence:
Bacterial disease:
Branch tip wilting, leaves turning black and drying out, is a typical manifestation of bacterial pith necrosis — the bacteria enter through wounds, causing internal stem rot, blocking nutrient transport, and ultimately leading to branch tip dieback.
Physiological nutrient deficiency:
New leaves shriveling, uneven leaf color, is a manifestation of boron/calcium deficiency, which increases the fragility of branch tips and makes them more susceptible to bacterial infection.
Emergency treatment steps:
Remove diseased branches to clear the source:
Cut off the wilted branch tips (cut 5cm above the diseased area), seal and discard the diseased branches, disinfect scissors with alcohol to avoid cross-contamination.
Spray fungicide:
Mix thiazole zinc 800x solution + zhongsheng mycin 600x solution and spray, focusing on stems and new shoots, once every 7 days for 2 consecutive applications.
Nutrient supplementation for recovery:
Spray liquid boron + sugar alcohol calcium 1000x solution on leaf surface, once every 10 days for 2 consecutive applications, to enhance branch tip resistance; at the same time control watering to prevent waterlogging in the pot.

It is quite severe, cannot be guaranteed

Can it eliminate this weed?

Yes! 50% Benazolin is very effective against this type of weed (by its appearance it looks like Veronica / Lamium, a broadleaf weed), it is one of its target weeds.
Key points:
Benazolin specifically controls broadleaf weeds such as chickweed, cleavers, Veronica, Lamium, shepherd's purse, etc., with high activity against these weeds.
Optimal application timing: Weed 2-4 leaf stage (the size in your photo is just right), fava bean 3-5 true leaf stage.
Dosage reference: 50% Benazolin emulsifiable concentrate 30-40 mL/mu, mix with 30-40 kg of water for uniform spray, leaves should be wet but not dripping.
Note: Avoid fava bean flowering period; do not mix with 2,4-D or MCPA; if there are rapeseed, Chinese cabbage and other crucifer crops nearby, ensure proper isolation to prevent drift phytotoxicity.
Effectiveness assessment:
Under normal weather conditions (10-25°C), weeds will start to yellow and wilt 7-10 days after application, and gradually die within about 15 days; it will be slower at low temperatures.
When should the pesticide be applied? ; What leaf stage?

May I ask what crop you are using it for? ; Rapeseed 4-leaf stage to pre-budding is the optimal safe window for using 50% Benazolin, and is also the key period for controlling broadleaf weeds.
Core usage points:
Optimal timing: Rapeseed 4-6 leaf stage, weeds at 2-4 leaf stage. At this point rapeseed resistance is strongest, weeds are small and easy to control, best results and safest.
Safety boundaries:
Earliest: Not before rapeseed 3-leaf stage, applying too early can easily cause phytotoxicity.
Latest: Must be applied before rapeseed begins to bud. Strictly prohibited after budding and during flowering, otherwise it will cause flowers without fruit, malformed pods, and severe yield reduction.
Dosage reference (50% Benazolin emulsifiable concentrate):
Normal dosage: 30-40ml/mu, mix with 30-40 kg of water for uniform spray.
When weed density is high or weed age is older, can increase to 40-50ml/mu, but strictly do not exceed the dosage.
Key prohibitions:
Strictly no mixing: Cannot be mixed with hormone-type herbicides such as 2,4-D and MCPA, this very easily causes severe phytotoxicity.
Environmental requirements: Apply on clear windless days, temperature at 10-25°C. Both low temperature (<8°C) and high temperature (>28°C) can easily cause phytotoxicity, and effectiveness is poor.
Drift risk: Pay attention to wind direction during application, avoid spray drift onto adjacent sensitive crops such as fava beans, peas, Chinese cabbage, radishes, etc.
Phytotoxicity risk warning:
If applied before 3-leaf stage or after budding, or if overdosed or applied at high temperature, rapeseed may show symptoms of leaf shriveling, yellowing, and slow growth, which in severe cases affects yield.

Can it be used on mustard-type rapeseed?

50% Benazolin can be used on mustard-type rapeseed, but the application timing, dosage, and conditions must be strictly controlled to avoid phytotoxicity.
Core usage points (precisely adapted for mustard-type rapeseed):
Applicable timing: Can only be used during mustard-type rapeseed 4-6 leaf stage, when the rapeseed has the strongest pesticide tolerance; seedlings too small (below 3 leaves) or too large (above 7 leaves) will greatly increase the risk of phytotoxicity, absolutely prohibited after bolting.
Application dosage: 50% Benazolin emulsifiable concentrate, 30-40ml per mu, mix with 30-40 kg of water for uniform spray, strictly do not add more at will (adding more easily causes rapeseed leaves to yellow and growth to stagnate).
Target weeds: Mainly targets broadleaf weeds in rapeseed fields, such as chickweed, giant chickweed, cleavers, water chickweed, etc., has no effect on grass weeds; if there are many grass weeds in the field, they need to be combined with Sethoxydim and other agents.
Key precautions (core for avoiding phytotoxicity):
Temperature requirements: Daily average temperature must be 8-25°C when applying. Below 8°C the rapeseed metabolism is slow and the chemical can accumulate and cause phytotoxicity (leaf whitening, shriveling); avoid midday during hot, strong-sun days as well.
Soil conditions: If soil is dry, lightly water first before applying, otherwise weed resistance is strong, control effect is poor, and rapeseed is more susceptible to phytotoxicity; best results when soil is moist.
Mixing prohibitions: Cannot be mixed or alternated in the short term with hormone-type herbicides such as MCPA and halosulfuron-methyl, to avoid increasing phytotoxicity risk.
Next crop effects: If the next crop to be planted is cotton, soybeans, peanuts and other broadleaf crops, an interval of more than 60 days is required to avoid residual phytotoxicity.
Emergency phytotoxicity treatment:
If rapeseed yellowing and slow growth occurs due to overdose or improper seedling age, promptly spray brassinolide + amino acid foliar fertilizer, once every 7 days, for 2 consecutive applications, to help alleviate phytotoxicity and promote recovery.
Has the tomato already started curling its leaves? Will this pesticide be effective?

Tomato leaf curl can be either physiological or pathological. First identify the type then treat accordingly for faster results👇
I. First quickly identify (core distinction)
Physiological leaf curl (most common): Entire plant / multiple plants with leaves curling up or down, no disease spots, no insect frass, mostly due to environment / management issues
Pathological leaf curl: Local leaf curling, accompanied by yellowing, spots, deformity, insect holes / frass, mostly viral disease / insect pests
II. Physiological leaf curl (first adjust environment + management)
1. High temperature strong light / drought (leaves curling up, brittle)
Shade: Use shade net (30%-50%) at noon, avoid direct sunlight
Water: Water thoroughly morning and evening, keep soil moist but not waterlogged; spray clean water on leaf surface to cool (avoid noon)
Loosen soil: Lightly loosen surface soil to enhance soil aeration
2. Overwatering / waterlogging (leaves curling down, yellowing, root rot)
Reduce water: Stop watering, loosen soil for ventilation, speed up soil drying
Save roots: When root rot is severe, remove from pot and cut off rotten roots, disinfect with carbendazim, replant in loose soil
3. Improper fertilization (fertilizer damage / nutrient deficiency)
Fertilizer damage (leaf edges scorched, curling): Flush pot soil with lots of water to dilute fertilizer; stop fertilizing for 1-2 weeks
Nutrient deficiency (potassium / boron deficiency, leaves wrinkling, deformed): Supplement potassium dihydrogen phosphate (foliar spray, 1:1000) + boron fertilizer, once every 7-10 days
4. Improper pruning / deshooting (top tender leaves curling)
Avoid removing too many lateral shoots at once, do it gradually; apply carbendazim to wounds after deshooting to prevent infection
III. Pathological leaf curl (targeted treatment)
1. Viral disease (leaves shriveling, yellow-green mottling, plant dwarfing)
No specific effective drug; first prevent transmission vectors: spray imidacloprid / acetamiprid to kill aphids, whiteflies
Treat diseased plants: Severely diseased plants should be pulled and burned to prevent spread; lightly diseased plants can be sprayed with amino-oligosaccharides / mushroom polysaccharides to enhance resistance
2. Insect pests (aphids / whiteflies / broad mites, insects / honeydew on leaf undersides)
Aphids / whiteflies: imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, once every 7 days, 2-3 consecutive applications
Broad mites (leaf undersides shiny, rigid, curling): abamectin, pyridaben, focus spray on leaf undersides
3. Fungal / bacterial disease (with disease spots, such as late blight, bacterial wilt)
Late blight (brown spots on leaves, white mold in moist conditions): propamocarb hydrochloride, fluazinam
Bacterial wilt (plant suddenly wilts, vascular bundles in stem turn brown): thiazole zinc, kasugamycin, root irrigation + foliar spray
IV. General prevention (reduce recurrence)
Maintain ventilation and light, avoid leaves staying wet for long periods
Regularly spray protective fungicides (mancozeb, chlorothalonil), once every 7-10 days
Promptly remove diseased leaves and weeds to reduce insect and fungal sources

The yellowing may also be caused by bacteria, which can spread, so trim some of the yellow leaves and use hydroponics treasure at the correct ratio, then it can be placed under light, which promotes root growth~

Can this be used?

Strawberry leaf edges turning black and drying out, the core cause is impaired root absorption + environment / management issues, prioritize checking these 4 points👇
1. Most common: Root system problems (root asphyxiation / root rot / weak roots)
Cause: Pot soil waterlogged, compacted, poor aeration, or watering too frequently, roots lack oxygen and rot, cannot absorb water, leaf tips / leaf edges turn black and dry out first.
Check: Pot soil consistently wet, with off-smell; pull out and check roots → black / soft and rotten.
Treatment:
Reduce water + loosen soil: Stop watering, lightly loosen surface soil, increase ventilation to speed up drying;
Serious: Remove from pot, cut rotten roots, disinfect with carbendazim, replant in loose soil (garden soil + leaf mold + perlite = 3:4:3).
2. Fertilizer damage (improper fertilization)
Cause: Concentrated fertilizer, raw fertilizer, too frequent fertilization, or high-concentration foliar fertilizer, burning roots / leaves, leaf edges turn black and scorched.
Check: Appears 1-3 days after fertilizing, leaves wrinkle, edges scorched and brittle.
Treatment:
Immediately flush pot soil with lots of water to dilute fertilizer;
Stop fertilizing for 1-2 months, apply thin fertilizer frequently after recovery.
3. Environmental dry heat / cold wind / direct sun
Cause: Dry air, temperature too high (>30°C), cold wind blowing directly (window / air conditioning outlet), sudden direct sunlight, leaves lose water, edges turn black.
Check: Multiple plants show symptoms simultaneously, no off-smell, pot soil moisture is normal.
Treatment:
Move to indirect light, good ventilation location, away from heat sources / cold wind;
Spray water around during the day to increase humidity (do not spray into the crown center).
4. Disease (anthracnose / leaf spot / blight)
Cause: High humidity, poor ventilation, fungal / bacterial infection, leaf edges turn black and spread into the leaf, with spots / rot.
Check: Leaves have brown spots, mold layer in moist conditions, gradually spreading.
Treatment:
Remove diseased leaves, dispose of them together;
Spray mancozeb / difenoconazole, once every 7-10 days, 2-3 consecutive applications.
Rapid troubleshooting steps:
Touch pot soil: Wet → reduce water, loosen soil; Dry → water thoroughly;
Recall: Recent fertilization / repotting / direct sunlight → treat accordingly;
Look at leaves: Insects / spots / mold → disease; None → environment / roots.
Subsequent care:
Watering: Water thoroughly when surface dry, do not let water sit in pot, avoid water accumulating in crown center;
Fertilizer: Apply thin fertilizer frequently, use strawberry-specific fertilizer, halve the concentration;
Environment: Maintain ventilation, temperature 15-25°C, avoid sudden temperature changes.
This flower keeps dropping new leaves as soon as they grow

Roses dropping new leaves as soon as they grow — the core cause is root system problems (root asphyxiation / root rot / weak roots), followed by sudden environmental changes, fertilizer damage, and disease/pest damage. Follow the troubleshooting below to quickly save them👇
I. Most core cause: Impaired root absorption (80% probability)
1. Root asphyxiation / root rot (most common)
Cause: Pot soil compacted, waterlogged, poor aeration, or watering too frequently, roots lack oxygen and rot, new leaves drop as soon as they grow because they cannot absorb water, old leaves also yellow.
Check: Pot soil consistently wet, with off-smell; pull out and check roots → black / soft and rotten.
Treatment:
Reduce water + loosen soil: Stop watering, lightly loosen surface soil (2-3cm), increase ventilation to speed up soil drying;
Serious: Remove from pot, cut rotten roots, disinfect with carbendazim, replant in loose soil (leaf mold + garden soil + perlite = 4:3:3), put ceramic particles / broken tiles at the bottom of the pot.
2. Weak roots / transplant failure
Cause: Newly bought / recently repotted roses with root systems not yet established, new leaves drop directly after consuming nutrients because they cannot absorb water.
Treatment:
Move to indirect light, well-ventilated location, avoid direct sunlight and cold wind;
Do not fertilize during transplant period, water sparingly, water again after surface soil dries, keep slightly moist, resume normal care after 1-2 weeks of establishment.
II. Other common causes
1. Sudden environmental changes (light / temperature / ventilation)
Cause: Suddenly moved from dark area to strong light, sudden temperature changes (< 10°C or > 30°C), poor ventilation, new leaves drop from stress.
Treatment:
Light: Gradually increase light exposure, first place in indirect light, then move to full sun after adaptation;
Temperature: Maintain 15-25°C, in winter keep away from window gaps, in summer provide 50% shade;
Ventilation: Open windows for ventilation 1-2 hours per day, avoid stuffiness.
2. Fertilizer damage (improper fertilization)
Cause: Concentrated fertilizer, raw fertilizer, too frequent fertilization, or fertilizing during transplant period, root burning causes new leaves to drop.
Treatment:
Immediately flush pot soil with lots of water (2-3 consecutive times) to dilute fertilizer;
Stop fertilizing for 1-2 months, then apply thin rose-specific fertilizer / potassium dihydrogen phosphate (1:1000) frequently after recovery.
3. Disease/pest damage (aphids / red spider mites / black spot disease)
Cause: Aphids, red spider mites sucking sap from new leaves, or black spot disease infection, causing new leaves to yellow and drop.
Check: Insects / honeydew on the underside of new leaves, or brown disease spots on leaves.
Treatment:
Insect pests: imidacloprid / abamectin, once every 7 days, 2-3 consecutive applications;
Disease: mancozeb / difenoconazole, focus spray on leaf undersides, once every 7-10 days.
III. Rapid troubleshooting steps:
Touch pot soil: Wet → reduce water, loosen soil; Dry → water thoroughly;
Recall: Recently bought / repotted / fertilized / moved → treat accordingly;
Look at leaves: Insects / disease spots → disease/pest damage; None → roots / environment.
IV. Subsequent care (prevent recurrence):
Watering: Water thoroughly when surface dry, do not let water sit in pot, do not let water accumulate in crown center;
Soil: Use loose, well-drained soil, lightly loosen surface soil monthly;
Fertilizer: Apply thin fertilizer frequently during growing season (spring and autumn), stop fertilizing in summer and winter;
Environment: Maintain ventilation and light, avoid sudden temperature changes.
Can my plant be saved in this condition?

Diagnosis: Your vegetable has powdery mildew
From the photo, the white powdery substance on the leaves is a typical symptom of powdery mildew. This is a common fungal disease that tends to break out in high humidity and poor ventilation environments.
🛠️ Emergency treatment steps:
Isolate diseased plants
Immediately separate the diseased plants from healthy ones to prevent fungal spores from spreading through the air.
Remove diseased leaves
Use clean scissors to cut off all leaves with white powder and dead branches, put them in a bag and throw them away, do not leave them in the pot.
Improve ventilation
Move the pot to a well-ventilated location with sufficient light, to prevent leaves from staying wet for long periods.
Chemical treatment
For home growing, you can use baking soda solution (1g baking soda + 1 liter of water + a few drops of dish soap), spray on both sides of the leaves once a week.
Or use thiophanate-methyl or triadimefon diluted according to instructions, 2-3 consecutive applications, 7 days apart.
Adjust watering method
Water directly into the soil, avoid getting leaves wet, reduce conditions for fungal growth.
💡 Daily prevention tips:
Avoid overcrowding plants, regularly prune old leaves, ensure ventilation and light.
Before rainy season or high humidity weather, apply a protective fungicide in advance (such as mancozeb).
When fertilizing, increase the proportion of phosphorus and potassium fertilizers to enhance plant disease resistance.
What disease is this, can this medicine treat it?

This is typical strawberry powdery mildew (fruit surface turning white, grey, mold-like), which is a fungal disease. It must be dealt with immediately, otherwise it will infect the whole plant, cause fruit rot and reduce yield👇
I. Emergency treatment (stop the damage first)
Remove diseased fruit
Cut off this diseased fruit together with the fruit stem, take it outside the garden and throw away / bury it deeply. Do not leave it in the pot or field to prevent spore spread.
Spray the whole plant (key)
Use pyraclostrobin (Cabrio) + difenoconazole (or tebuconazole) mixed spray, dilute according to instructions, focus spray on fruit surface, calyx, leaf undersides, once every 7-10 days, 2-3 consecutive applications.
Other effective agents against powdery mildew include azoxystrobin, trifloxystrobin, fluopyram, etc.
Improve environment
Immediately enhance ventilation to reduce humidity; water only into the soil, not on fruit or leaves, avoid water accumulating on leaf centers / fruit surfaces.
II. Subsequent prevention (avoid recurrence)
Regular prevention: When not yet diseased, spray mancozeb, chlorothalonil or low-concentration pyraclostrobin once every 7-10 days to form a protective barrier.
Control humidity and ventilation: Strawberry powdery mildew loves high humidity (humidity > 80%) + poor ventilation; maintain good air circulation, don't allow stuffiness.
Reasonable fertilization: Apply more phosphorus and potassium fertilizers, less nitrogen fertilizer, enhance plant disease resistance.
Clean the field: Promptly remove old leaves, diseased leaves, diseased fruit to reduce fungal sources.
III. Quick identification:
Fruit surface white / grayish-white powdery substance, grey, no rot but loses commercial value → Powdery mildew (this is what you have)
Fruit surface brown rot, with mold layer, soft rot → Gray mold disease (different treatment, need to distinguish)
Can this medicine treat this disease?
No, it cannot; this one is for prevention
Which one can treat it?
Pyraclostrobin (Cabrio)

What is the dosage ratio?

For leaves with serious bacterial infection showing yellowing, powdery mildew, black spots etc., you can first trim them appropriately to prevent bacterial spread and nutrient competition, then mix 1g with 1 jin of water, spray the fungicide on the leaf surface, use once every 5 days or so, improvement will appear after 3-4 application cycles
Which medicine for curling leaves?

Tomato leaf curl can be either physiological or pathological. First identify the type then treat accordingly for faster results👇
I. First quickly identify (core distinction)
Physiological leaf curl (most common): Entire plant / multiple plants with leaves curling up or down, no disease spots, no insect frass, mostly due to environment / management issues
Pathological leaf curl: Local leaf curling, accompanied by yellowing, spots, deformity, insect holes / frass, mostly viral disease / insect pests
II. Physiological leaf curl (first adjust environment + management)
1. High temperature strong light / drought (leaves curling up, brittle)
Shade: Use shade net (30%-50%) at noon, avoid direct sunlight
Water: Water thoroughly morning and evening, keep soil moist but not waterlogged; spray clean water on leaf surface to cool (avoid noon)
Loosen soil: Lightly loosen surface soil to enhance soil aeration
2. Overwatering / waterlogging (leaves curling down, yellowing, root rot)
Reduce water: Stop watering, loosen soil for ventilation, speed up soil drying
Save roots: When root rot is severe, remove from pot and cut off rotten roots, disinfect with carbendazim, replant in loose soil
3. Improper fertilization (fertilizer damage / nutrient deficiency)
Fertilizer damage (leaf edges scorched, curling): Flush pot soil with lots of water to dilute fertilizer; stop fertilizing for 1-2 weeks
Nutrient deficiency (potassium / boron deficiency, leaves wrinkling, deformed): Supplement potassium dihydrogen phosphate (foliar spray, 1:1000) + boron fertilizer, once every 7-10 days
4. Improper pruning / deshooting (top tender leaves curling)
Avoid removing too many lateral shoots at once, do it gradually; apply carbendazim to wounds after deshooting to prevent infection
III. Pathological leaf curl (targeted treatment)
1. Viral disease (leaves shriveling, yellow-green mottling, plant dwarfing)
No specific effective drug; first prevent transmission vectors: spray imidacloprid / acetamiprid to kill aphids, whiteflies
Treat diseased plants: Severely diseased plants should be pulled and burned to prevent spread; lightly diseased plants can be sprayed with amino-oligosaccharides / mushroom polysaccharides to enhance resistance
2. Insect pests (aphids / whiteflies / broad mites, insects / honeydew on leaf undersides)
Aphids / whiteflies: imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, once every 7 days, 2-3 consecutive applications
Broad mites (leaf undersides shiny, rigid, curling): abamectin, pyridaben, focus spray on leaf undersides
3. Fungal / bacterial disease (with disease spots, such as late blight, bacterial wilt)
Late blight (brown spots on leaves, white mold in moist conditions): propamocarb hydrochloride, fluazinam
Bacterial wilt (plant suddenly wilts, vascular bundles in stem turn brown): thiazole zinc, kasugamycin, root irrigation + foliar spray
IV. General prevention (reduce recurrence)
Maintain ventilation and light, avoid leaves staying wet for long periods
Regularly spray protective fungicides (mancozeb, chlorothalonil), once every 7-10 days
Promptly remove diseased leaves and weeds to reduce insect and fungal sources
How to use it?

Then mix 1g with 1 jin of water and spray on the leaf surface, use once every 5 days or so, increase the amount based on field size, 500g per mu, improvement will appear after 3-4 application cycles.
